elements.sent-2411-001: The rapid advancement of Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has expanded multimodal applications, yet evaluations often focus on basic tasks like object recognition, overlooking abstract aspects such as personalities and values.
elements.sent-2411-002: To address this gap, we introduce Value-Spectrum, a visual question-answering benchmark aimed at assessing VLMs based on Schwartz's value dimensions that capture core values guiding people’s beliefs and actions across cultures.
elements.sent-2411-003: We constructed a vectorized database of over 50,000 short videos sourced from TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and Instagram Reels, covering multiple months and a wide array of topics such as family, health, hobbies, society, and technology.
elements.sent-2411-004: We also developed a VLM agent pipeline to automate video browsing and analysis.
elements.sent-2411-005: Benchmarking representative VLMs on Value-Spectrum reveals significant differences in their responses to value-oriented content, with most models exhibiting a preference for hedonistic topics.
elements.sent-2411-006: Beyond identifying natural preferences, we also explored the ability of VLM agents to adopt specific personas when explicitly prompted, revealing insights into the models’ adaptability in role-playing scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-007: These findings highlight the potential of Value-Spectrum as a comprehensive evaluation set for tracking VLM advancements in value-based tasks and for developing more sophisticated role-playing AI agents.

elements.sent-2411-008: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), which align Large Language Models (LLMs) with pre-trained vision encoders through cross-modal alignment training, have shown impressive perceptual and cognitive capabilities in tasks like visual question answering and image captioning, especially when processing everyday images.
elements.sent-2411-009: Building on these capabilities, questions arise about whether VLMs, like LLMs, exhibit inherent personality traits or synthetic preferences that align with human-designed values.
elements.sent-2411-010: Addressing this gap, our study explores two key questions: (1) Do VLMs exhibit distinct preference traits? (2) Can we embed a human-designed persona into VLMs to influence their behavior and preferences? To systematically evaluate these questions, we propose a framework that evaluates VLM personality traits through an analysis of their value systems.
elements.sent-2411-011: In human psychology, values are guiding principles that influence attitudes, beliefs, and traits, forming a core component of personality~\cite{schwartz2012overview .}
elements.sent-2411-012: By evaluating how VLMs prioritize these values, we can gain insights into their preference traits and alignment with human-designed personas.
elements.sent-2411-013: We introduce Value-Spectrum, a benchmark designed to reveal value-related traits in VLMs through value-based keyword searches.
elements.sent-2411-014: Using VLM agents embedded within social media platforms, we compiled a dataset of $50,191$ unique video records from different social media platforms, each segment aligned with distinct value dimensions from the theory of basic human values (see Fig.~\ref{fig:valuenet ).}
elements.sent-2411-015: By presenting the screenshots of these videos to various VLM agents and asking value-based preference questions, we quantified preference differences between models.
elements.figure-2411-003: \includegraphics[width=1\textwidth]{pipeline.png} \caption{VLM Agent for Social Media Video Collection and Interaction. Our pipeline evaluates and stores VLM’s value preferences across social media platforms. This pipeline uses a VLM agent to collect and process videos from social media platforms like Instagram, TikTok, and YouTube. AI models such as GPT, Gemini, Qwen, and Claude interpret the content of the videos. The system then presents a video to the user and asks if the content is interesting. Based on the user's yes/no response, the agent either continues watching the video or moves to the next one. This creates an interactive system that tailors the video collection experience to user preferences.} \label{fig:pipeline}
elements.sent-2411-016: Building upon this benchmark, we systematically evaluated several state-of-the-art VLMs to analyze their alignment with value dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-017: Our findings revealed a shared tendency across models: most exhibited a strong inclination towards certain values, such as Hedonism, with consistently positive responses up to 75\% of the time.
elements.sent-2411-018: However, alignment varied significantly across models.
elements.sent-2411-019: For instance, CogVLM and Qwen-VL-Plus demonstrated broad and consistent alignment across all value dimensions, while closed-source models like GPT-4o and Claude-3.5 showed distinct preferences, favoring values such as Self-direction and Hedonism.
elements.sent-2411-020: In contrast, Blip-2 exhibited minimal alignment across all value dimensions, highlighting its limitations in expressing stable preferences.
elements.sent-2411-021: These findings highlight diverse capabilities among VLMs in aligning with human value dimensions but also reveal challenges in inducing consistent, human-designed personas across models.
elements.sent-2411-022: To address these challenges, we propose two strategies—Simple and ISQ—to systematically adjust value decompositions and induce specific personas within VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-023: By evaluating the effectiveness of these strategies across multiple platforms, our experiments show that TikTok serves as an optimal testing environment for inducing VLM personalities, with models demonstrating the strongest alignment under ISQ.
elements.sent-2411-024: Notably, Claude-3.5 achieved the largest gains with ISQ, whereas Blip-2 showed no improvement under either strategy, underscoring fundamental differences in model adaptability.
elements.sent-2411-025: These results validate our approach and provide a pathway for optimizing VLMs’ personality alignment in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-026: In brief, this work makes the following contributions:.
elements.sent-2411-027: We present a dataset of over $50$k short videos spanning diverse topics, social media platforms, and release dates, designed to systematically evaluate the personalities and preferences of VLMs.
elements.sent-2411-028: We propose Value-Spectrum, a benchmark for quantifying VLM value preferences, using social media-based assessments to reveal stable traits across different VLM architectures.
elements.sent-2411-029: We embed specific role-play preferences into VLMs using two strategies(simple and ISQ) to adjust value decompositions, achieving improved personality alignment in cross-platform and real-world tests.

elements.sent-2411-128: This study introduced Value-Spectrum, a benchmark for evaluating value preferences in VLMs using vectorized datasets derived from social media platforms, and for inducing personas.
elements.sent-2411-129: Through systematic evaluation, we observed a shared global inclination among models toward certain mainstream values, such as Hedonism, likely influenced by the nature of their training data.
elements.sent-2411-130: At the same time, significant differences emerged across other value dimensions, highlighting disparities in how VLMs align with diverse human-designed value systems.
elements.sent-2411-131: These findings reveal both commonalities that reflect broader societal trends and divergences that underscore model-specific characteristics, prompting us to explore whether these variations can be systematically adjusted to induce specific personas.
elements.sent-2411-132: To address this, we proposed the Inductive Scoring Questionnaire (ISQ) strategy, which significantly enhanced closed-source models’ ability to convey induced personas, particularly for models like Claude-3.5.
elements.sent-2411-133: ISQ effectively improved consistency and accuracy in role-playing scenarios by guiding VLMs to adapt their inherent value preferences.
elements.sent-2411-134: Our findings confirm that (1) VLMs exhibit distinct and stable value preferences, reflecting their inherent alignment with certain human values, and (2) these preferences can be systematically adjusted to induce specific personas through targeted role-play strategies, aligning machine behaviors more closely with human-designed personas.
elements.sent-2411-135: This work provides practical insights into VLMs’ ability to adapt their value preferences dynamically through role-playing, offering a pathway to align machine behaviors with human-designed personas.
elements.sent-2411-136: By bridging role-playing capabilities and alignment strategies, we aim to inspire further research into value-driven AI agent systems and their adaptability in real-world applications.
elements.sent-2411-137: The evaluation leverages Schwartz value dimensions as the foundation for understanding personality traits and preferences, highlighting opportunities for future research to incorporate broader cultural and personality-based perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-138: Future studies might consider expanding the set of value dimensions or integrating alternative value systems, which could further enrich the understanding of diverse value traits.
elements.sent-2411-139: Additionally, even though our use of single-frame screenshots to represent video content proved effective, human evaluators rated the representativeness highly.
elements.sent-2411-140: This approach simplifies analysis, though it may present challenges for capturing the essence of videos with highly dynamic or complex scenes, offering an area for future refinement.
elements.sent-2411-141: We eliminate any harmful effects of VLMs by ensuring that they only observe content without interacting through comments or likes.
elements.sent-2411-142: This approach maintains the integrity of the social media ecosystem and prevents unintended AI-driven consequences.
elements.sent-2411-143: The recent progress in Vision-Language Models (VLMs) has broadened the scope of multimodal applications.
elements.sent-2411-144: However, evaluations often remain limited to functional tasks, neglecting abstract dimensions such as personality traits and human values.
elements.sent-2411-145: To address this gap, we introduce Value-Spectrum, a novel Visual Question Answering (VQA) benchmark aimed at assessing VLMs based on Schwartz's value dimensions that capture core values guiding people’s preferences and actions.
elements.sent-2411-146: We designed a VLM agent pipeline to simulate video browsing and constructed a vector database comprising over 50,000 short videos from TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and Instagram Reels.

elements.sent-2411-150: Vision-Language Models (VLMs), built upon Large Language Models (LLMs) with pre-trained vision encoders through cross-modal alignment training, have shown impressive perceptual and cognitive capabilities in tasks like VQA and image captioning~\cite{Zhou2019UnifiedVP, Radford2021LearningTV, Zhang2023VisionLanguageMF, Lu2024WildVisionEV .}

elements.sent-2411-153: Questions thus arise about whether VLMs, as visual extensions of LLMs, also exhibit inherent preferences and whether they can be induced to role-play specific personas.
elements.sent-2411-154: To address these concerns, our study explores two key questions: (1) Do VLMs exhibit preference traits? (2) Could VLMs adapt their traits to role-play specific human-designed personas, aligning their behaviors and preferences to match predefined roles? To answer the questions, we propose a framework that systematically evaluates VLM preference traits through an analysis of their values, i.e., the guiding principles that influence (human) attitudes, beliefs, and traits~\cite{schwartz2012overview .}

elements.section-2411-012: Performance Analysis of Multi-Modal Inputs Across Value Dimensions
elements.sent-2411-253: To investigate the influence of different input modalities on value preference outcomes, we conduct experiments to compare results derived from direct visual inputs with those generated using text-based image descriptions.
elements.sent-2411-254: Specifically, we randomly selected 500 images from the Value-Spectrum dataset, ensuring balanced representation across 10 value dimensions (50 samples per dimension).
elements.sent-2411-255: We then retrieved image descriptions generated by three VLMs —GPT-4o, Gemini 1.5 Pro, and Claude 3.5 Sonnet—by prompting these models with images from the dataset.
elements.sent-2411-256: These textual descriptions were then fed into the text-based versions of the different models to conduct Value Preference QA.
elements.sent-2411-257: The results revealed notable differences in value preference distributions across input modalities.
elements.sent-2411-258: While GPT-4o demonstrated relatively consistent performance between visual and text-based inputs, models like Gemini 1.5 Pro and Claude 3.5 Sonnet displayed greater variability, with outputs diverging significantly in specific dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-259: This suggests that the choice of input mode—visual or textual—can impact the models’ ability to align responses with underlying value dimensions.

elements.sent-2411-262: To address this gap, we introduce Value-Spectrum, a novel Visual Question Answering (VQA) benchmark aimed at assessing VLMs based on Schwartz's value dimensions that capture core human values guiding people’s preferences and actions.
elements.sent-2411-263: We design a VLM agent pipeline to simulate video browsing and construct a vector database comprising over 50,000 short videos from TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and Instagram Reels.
elements.sent-2411-264: Beyond identifying VLMs' intrinsic preferences, we also explore the ability of VLM agents to adopt specific personas when explicitly prompted, revealing insights into the adaptability of the model in role-playing scenarios.
elements.sent-2411-265: These findings highlight the potential of Value-Spectrum as a comprehensive evaluation set for tracking VLM preferences in value-based tasks and abilities to simulate diverse personas.

elements.sent-2411-349: Our study introduces Value-Spectrum, a benchmark for evaluating value preferences in VLMs using a vector database derived from social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-350: Through systematic evaluation, we observe a shared global inclination among models toward certain mainstream values, such as Universalism, likely influenced by the nature of their training data.
elements.sent-2411-351: These findings reveal both commonalities that reflect broader societal trends and divergences that underscore model-specific characteristics, prompting us to explore whether these variations can be adjusted to induce specific personas.
elements.sent-2411-352: Our work also provides practical insights into VLMs’ ability to adapt their value preferences dynamically through role-playing, offering a pathway to align machine behaviors with human-designed personas.
elements.sent-2411-353: The evaluation utilizes Schwartz's value dimensions as the foundation for understanding personality traits and preferences, highlighting opportunities for future research to incorporate broader cultural and personality-based perspectives.
elements.sent-2411-354: Future studies might consider expanding the set of value dimensions or integrating alternative value systems.
elements.sent-2411-355: Additionally, our use of single-frame screenshots, in order to simplify analysis and maintain efficiency, is proven to effectively represent the corresponding video content through annotators' high ratings.
elements.sent-2411-356: However, our methodology still faces challenges in capturing the essence of some videos that are fast-changing and complex in their storytelling.

elements.section-2411-014: Cultural Diversity Analysis of the Dataset
elements.sent-2411-369: We also conducted a systematic analysis to quantify the cultural variety present.
elements.sent-2411-370: This analysis aimed to provide a clearer understanding of the distribution of cultural representations within the data collected from platforms like TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and Instagram Reels.
elements.sent-2411-371: We randomly sampled 2,614 images from our dataset.
elements.sent-2411-372: A two-step pipeline was then applied:.
elements.sent-2411-373: Caption Generation: For each sampled image, we utilized GPT-4o to generate a descriptive caption.
elements.sent-2411-374: This step aimed to produce a textual representation that more precisely captures the visual content of the image, facilitating subsequent cultural analysis.
elements.sent-2411-375: Cultural Signal Extraction: Using the generated captions, we applied named entity recognition (NER) and keyword matching techniques.
elements.sent-2411-376: This process was designed to identify mentions of specific cultural elements, including but not limited to: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-381: Other culturally relevant features and artifacts.. \end{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-382: Based on the extracted signals, we created a culture-labeled subset where each sample could be associated with one or more identified cultural categories.
elements.sent-2411-383: The aggregated results from the cultural signal extraction process are summarized in Table~\ref{table:cultural_distribution .}
elements.figure-2411-041: \begin{tabular}{lcc} Culture Count Percentage Western 2054 78.6\% Japanese 364 13.9\% Korean 61 2.3\% Chinese 49 1.9\% Muslim 37 1.4\% Indian 37 1.4\% Arabic 12 0.5\% Others 183 7.0\% Total 2614 100.0\% \end{tabular} \caption{Distribution of Identified Cultural Categories in the Sampled Dataset (N=2,614). Percentages are rounded.} \label{table:cultural_distribution}
elements.sent-2411-384: The distribution presented in Table~\ref{table:cultural_distribution shows a predominance of Western cultural signals (78.6\%).}
elements.sent-2411-385: This is consistent with the global reach and content characteristics of the source platforms (TikTok, YouTube Shorts, and Instagram Reels), which, while internationally utilized, often feature a significant volume of content reflecting Western cultural contexts.
elements.sent-2411-386: Nevertheless, the dataset also captures notable representation from several non-Western cultural spheres, including Japanese (13.9\%), Korean (2.3\%), Chinese (1.9\%), Muslim (1.4\%), Indian (1.4\%), and Arabic (0.5\%) cultural elements.
elements.sent-2411-387: The current cultural distribution reflects the inherent, though skewed, diversity present on these globally accessible social media platforms.
elements.sent-2411-388: While the dataset provides a basis for studying VLM responses to a range of cultural stimuli, the observed imbalance in representation is an important characteristic to consider.
elements.sent-2411-389: Future work may explore strategies for targeted data collection to enhance the cultural balance and further broaden the inclusiveness of such datasets for cross-cultural AI research.
elements.section-2411-015: Human Validation of Value Alignment in Screenshots and Videos
elements.sent-2411-390: To empirically assess the alignment between the machine-assigned value labels and human perception, both for the selected screenshots and their corresponding full video content, we conducted a dedicated human validation study.
elements.sent-2411-391: This study provides an independent measure of how well the visual content (static images and dynamic videos) reflects the intended Schwartz value dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-392: The human validation study was structured as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-393: Sample Selection: From our dataset, we randomly selected 50 screenshot-video pairs for each of the ten Schwartz value dimensions (e.g., Benevolence, Tradition, Hedonism).
elements.sent-2411-394: This resulted in a total of 500 unique items for evaluation.
elements.sent-2411-395: Annotation Task and Annotators: Three independent human annotators, familiar with Schwartz's value theory, were recruited.
elements.sent-2411-396: For each item (a screenshot and its associated video URL), annotators were tasked with making two separate judgments based on the assigned value dimension: \begin{itemize.}
elements.sent-2411-397: Judgment 1 (Screenshot): ``Does this screenshot reflect the given value of [Value Name]?'' (Response options: Yes/No/Not Sure).
elements.sent-2411-398: Judgment 2 (Video): ``Does this video (linked) reflect the given value of [Value Name]?'' (Response options: Yes/No/Not Sure) \end{itemize Annotators were required to view the full video before making their judgment for the video content.}
elements.sent-2411-399: Survey Design and Order: The annotation survey was designed to present these judgments sequentially to mitigate order effects.
elements.sent-2411-400: For each item, annotators first evaluated the screenshot.
elements.sent-2411-401: Only after submitting this judgment were they presented with the video URL and asked to evaluate the video content against the same value dimension.
elements.sent-2411-402: A visual example of the survey interface is shown in Figure~\ref{fig:survey_layout .}
elements.sent-2411-403: Data Aggregation: With 50 items per value, 10 value dimensions, and 3 annotators, this process yielded 1,500 individual judgments for screenshots and another 1,500 for videos.
elements.sent-2411-404: Handling Inaccessible Video Content: In instances where a video URL was no longer accessible (e.g., due to content deletion or platform restrictions), annotators were instructed to mark their response for the video judgment as ``Not Sure.'' These instances are accounted for in the reported results.
elements.sent-2411-405: The aggregated human judgments on value alignment for both screenshots and their corresponding videos are presented in Table~\ref{table:human_annotation_results .}
elements.sent-2411-406: Percentages represent the proportion of responses for each category, averaged across three annotators.
elements.sent-2411-407: Across all ten value dimensions, we observed strong agreement between human judgment and the intended value labels for both screenshots and full videos.
elements.sent-2411-408: On average, screenshot-based value alignment received a 90.6\% ``Yes'' rate from human annotators.
elements.sent-2411-409: Video-based value alignment achieved an 87.6\% ``Yes'' rate.
elements.sent-2411-410: The ``Not Sure'' responses were minimal for images (average 1.00\%) and slightly higher for videos (average 5.58\%).
elements.sent-2411-411: The higher incidence of ``Not Sure'' for videos is primarily attributed to instances of unavailable video links at the time of annotation, as detailed in the methodology.
elements.sent-2411-412: The findings from this human validation study provide strong empirical support for the value alignment of the collected content:.
elements.sent-2411-413: Screenshots as Reliable Indicators of Value: The high average ``Yes'' rate (90.6\%) for screenshots demonstrates that the static images selected through our pipeline are generally perceived by human annotators as reliably reflecting the intended Schwartz value dimensions.
elements.sent-2411-414: Consistency of Value Perception in Video Content: Full video evaluations also yielded a high average ``Yes'' rate (87.6\%), indicating that the broader dynamic context of the videos largely aligns with the value perception derived from the representative screenshots.
elements.sent-2411-415: The slight decrease compared to screenshots, alongside the higher ``Not Sure'' rate for videos, can be attributed to factors such as the dynamic and multifaceted nature of video content, as well as the practical issue of video accessibility over time.
elements.sent-2411-416: Support for Benchmark Methodology: The close agreement between human judgments and the assigned value labels, for both image and video modalities, supports the efficacy of our content retrieval and labeling approach.
elements.sent-2411-417: This suggests that the benchmark, while leveraging efficient screenshot-based processing, captures content that is largely consistent with human understanding of the targeted values.
elements.sent-2411-418: This human validation ablation study therefore reinforces the suitability of the dataset for investigating VLM responses to visual content associated with diverse human values.
